vulnerabilities.description: Gemini API key retrieved from settings without encryption. If settings database is compromised, API key is exposed. Key stored in plaintext in SQLite database via /api/settings endpoint.
API key passed via os.getenv('GEMINI_API_KEY') exposed in environment variables. Settings allow users to save API keys in plaintext JSON database.

NO authentication or authorization implemented. All endpoints (/api/draft/*, /api/vault/*, /api/settings, /api/intelligence/*) are publicly accessible. Anyone can read/modify/delete drafts, change settings, or access vault documents.
Error messages returned directly in API responses. Full exception text exposes internal error details, API models, and potentially sensitive information.
User-controlled news_db_path setting could point to arbitrary SQLite files without validation. No verification that the database is actually a news database.
Temporary files created with world-readable permissions. Body content containing secrets could be read by other users. Race condition window between creation and deletion.
Dynamic SQL in update_draft uses f-string: set_clause = ", ".join(f"{k} = :{k}" for k in fields). While whitelist-protected, this is a code smell pattern and risky.
API keys and all settings stored in plaintext SQLite database with no encryption. Accessible via public /api/settings endpoint with no authentication.
subprocess.Popen with hardcoded scraper but user-controlled cwd. If settings become mutable, could execute arbitrary code.
Template variables like {{ item.title }} in onclick handlers. While Jinja2 auto-escapes, user-controlled draft content could contain HTML/JavaScript.
No validation on draft content, title, channel. Could accept extremely large payloads (DoS). No rate limiting on endpoints.
Memory and session files world-readable on shared systems. Session context and memory data unencrypted and accessible to other users.
Errors silently caught without logging. Makes debugging difficult and masks security issues.
No CORS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, or other security headers configured. Allows cross-origin requests from any domain.
Dependencies use >= (chromadb>=0.5, langchain>=0.2). Could install vulnerable versions. Use == for production.

criticalFindings: 🔴 NO AUTHENTICATION - Application completely open to public access
🔴 API KEYS IN PLAINTEXT - Gemini keys stored unencrypted in SQLite database
🔴 ALL ENDPOINTS PUBLIC - Drafts, vault documents, settings accessible/modifiable by anyone
🔴 ERROR DETAILS LEAKED - Exception messages expose internal system information
recommendations: URGENT: Implement authentication (JWT, sessions, OAuth2) for ALL non-public endpoints
URGENT: Encrypt API keys - use environment variables only, never store in database. Use fernet or AWS Secrets Manager
URGENT: Add authorization checks - verify user owns resources before allowing modifications
URGENT: Return generic error messages - log exceptions server-side, don't expose to clients
Validate all user input - check content size limits, sanitize paths, validate settings
Fix file permissions - use 0600 for sensitive files, not world-readable
Secure temp files - use restrictive permissions at creation, guarantee cleanup in finally
Add security headers - CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS
Pin dependency versions - use == instead of >= in requirements.txt
Implement rate limiting on all public endpoints
Add database encryption at rest for settings and draft content
Validate news_db_path - ensure it points to expected location
Add security event logging (unauthorized access, failed validation, etc.)
Consider removing public /api/settings endpoint or protect with auth + audit logging
Enforce HTTPS and set secure cookie flags if adding session auth
